subject: Mathematics
contextNote: Sec 2 Math, 24 students. You modelled operations with negative numbers using a number line — showing that adding a positive means moving right and subtracting a positive means moving left. You worked through −3 + 5 = 2 explicitly on the board. Students are now 21 minutes into a set of operations. The room is silent — not the productive hum of focused work, but the flat quiet of compliance without engagement.

students.name: Rajan
students.note: All questions correct. Sitting quietly, not asking for more work. No working shown — just answers.

students.name: Nur Atiqah
students.note: All questions correct. Head down, not looking up. Has not shown any working or number line use.

students.name: Wei Jie
students.note: Most questions correct but working slowly. Has drawn a number line for some questions but not all.

students.name: Siti
students.note: Several wrong answers — moving in the wrong direction on the number line. Has written −2 − 3 = 1 (moved right instead of left).

ep1.situation: 21 minutes into the lesson. You circulate and the silence is striking — not the productive hum of focused work, but the flat quiet of compliance without engagement. Rajan has all questions correct with only the final answers written — no working, no number line, no explanation of direction. He sits quietly, not asking for more work. Nur has all questions correct as well, head down, not looking up. She has not drawn a number line or shown any working. Wei Jie is working slowly and has drawn a number line for some questions but not all — his answers are mostly correct but he is not consistent about showing his reasoning. Siti has several wrong answers. She has written −2 − 3 = 1, suggesting she moved right instead of left when subtracting. The direction error is consistent across two questions. A quick scan of the class: 8 students have all correct answers with no working shown. 10 students have mostly correct answers with some working. 6 students, including Siti, have direction errors. The room is silent and compliant — but you cannot tell whether the silence masks understanding or confusion, and no one is talking, questioning, or justifying. You have about 19 minutes of lesson time remaining.
ep1.evidence: The silence reveals a participation gap, not just a readiness gap. Most students can do the operations correctly, but no one is talking about direction, reasoning, or the number line. The room is compliant but cognitively passive. Siti's direction errors suggest she has not internalised the left-right rule for negative operations.

choices.label: Thumbometer + two-step direction check
choices.elaboration: Stop the class and ask for a quick confidence check using thumbs: thumb up if you feel confident about the direction of movement on the number line, thumb sideways if you are unsure, thumb down if you are stuck. Then project a diagnostic question: '−2 − 3 = ?' Ask two questions: '(1) What is the answer?' Then: '(2) Which direction did you move on the number line? Choose: (a) Left, because subtracting means moving left. (b) Right, because the numbers are negative. (c) I did not use a number line. (d) I am not sure.' Ask students to write both answers on paper. Use the pattern to see who understands the direction rule and who is getting correct answers without using the number line.
choices.tradeoff: The thumbometer gives you a quick, low-stakes gauge of confidence without requiring students to speak. The two-step diagnostic reveals whether students understand the direction rule or are computing correctly by a different method. But the thumbometer may not be honest — quiet students may put thumbs sideways even if they understand, to avoid standing out. The diagnostic interrupts the few students who are working productively.

choices.label: Paired number line talk with ZPD prompts
choices.elaboration: Assign shoulder partners and ask them to work through the next question together using a number line. Give the instruction: 'Trace the operation on your number line and explain each step to your partner: Start at ___, move ___ because ___.' Distribute prompt cards to students who are stalled: 'Adding a positive number: move right. Subtracting a positive number: move left. Adding a negative number: move left. Subtracting a negative number: move right.' Circulate and listen for the quality of explanations. After 4 minutes, ask one or two pairs to share their explanation with the class.
choices.tradeoff: Paired number line work breaks the silence in a low-risk way and gives students a visual structure to reason through. The prompt cards support students with direction errors respectfully. But pair quality is uneven — if both students in a pair are silent or both have the same error, the pair will not be productive. Siti may copy her partner's number line without engaging with the direction reasoning.

choices.label: Accountable Talk: what does the operation mean on the number line?
choices.elaboration: Pause the class and introduce Accountable Talk stems. Write on the board: 'I claim the answer is ___ because ___. On the number line, I moved ___ because ___.' Ask students to turn to their shoulder partner and use the stem to explain their answer to the last question. The listener must respond: 'I agree because ___' or 'I disagree because ___.' Circulate and listen for the most common reasoning patterns and errors. After 3 minutes, select two contrasting pairs — one with clear direction reasoning, one with an error or shallow claim — and run a brief IRF probe: 'What did you notice about these two explanations? Which reasoning is stronger? Why?'
choices.tradeoff: Accountable Talk stems force students to make claims and justify them, not just share answers. The structure ensures reasoning is made explicit and breaks the silence in a structured way. But the stems may feel artificial or intimidating to quiet students. Siti may mumble the stem without genuine reasoning. Some students may say 'I agree' without actually evaluating their partner's claim.

A.consequence: The thumbs go up and the pattern is revealing. 8 students show thumbs up (confident), 12 show thumbs sideways (unsure), and 4 show thumbs down (stuck), including Siti. The thumbometer confirms what you suspected — most students are unsure, not confident. Then the diagnostic responses come in. 16 of 24 students got the correct answer (−5). But on the direction question, only 9 students chose option (a) — left, because subtracting means moving left. 7 students chose option (c) — they did not use a number line at all. 4 students chose option (b) — they moved right because the numbers are negative. Nur got the answer right but chose option (c) — she is computing correctly without the number line. Rajan got both right and chose option (a) with a clear explanation. Wei Jie got the answer right but chose option (c) — he is not consistently using the number line. Siti got the answer wrong and chose option (b) — she moved right because she associated negative numbers with rightward movement. The evidence is now clear: most students are computing correctly without the number line, and Siti has a specific direction misconception. 17 minutes remain.

choices.label: AQS (Affirm-Question-Suggest) peer feedback on direction reasoning
choices.elaboration: Use the thumbometer evidence to form pairs: students who showed thumbs up (like Rajan) are paired with students who showed thumbs sideways (like Nur and Wei Jie). Ask the confident student to give AQS feedback on their partner's diagnostic response: Affirm what is correct ('You got the right answer'), Question the direction ('You said you didn't use a number line — can you show me the direction on the number line?'), Suggest how to think about it ('Try drawing the number line and tracing the movement — subtracting always means moving left'). Circulate and listen for the quality of explanations. After 4 minutes, ask one pair to share their AQS exchange.
choices.tradeoff: AQS feedback gives quiet students structured practice in talking about their reasoning. The Affirm-Question-Suggest structure ensures feedback is specific and actionable. Pairing confident with unsure students leverages the expertise in the room. But the quality of peer explanation varies. Rajan may give minimal explanations because he is not used to articulating his reasoning. Siti, who has a direction misconception, needs her specific error addressed — not just a general prompt to use the number line.

choices.label: IRF (Initiate-Response-Feedback) chains on direction reasoning
choices.elaboration: Select 3–4 students who got both diagnostic questions right and can explain their direction reasoning. Run an IRF chain with the whole class: 'Why do we move left when we subtract? What is the reasoning?' Take the first student's response (Initiation). Then ask a second student: 'Can you add to what [first student] said?' (Response). Then ask a third student: 'Do you agree? Is there anything you would clarify or correct?' (Feedback). Build a complete, class-constructed explanation. Write the final reasoning on the board and ask all students to copy it into their notes.
choices.tradeoff: IRF chains build a shared explanation that is richer than any single student would produce. It makes the direction reasoning visible and public. But the chain is driven by the students who are already confident and can articulate their thinking. The majority of quiet students remain passive observers. Siti, who has a direction misconception, may copy the board explanation without correcting her underlying error.

B.consequence: The pairs begin working and the room shifts from silence to low murmurs. Rajan traces the operation on his number line and explains to his partner quietly: 'Start at −2, subtract 3 means move left 3 steps, land on −5.' His partner nods and traces the same movement. Nur is paired with another student who is also computing correctly without the number line. They trace the operation together and both confirm the answer — but neither student questions why subtracting means moving left. Wei Jie uses the prompt card and traces the operation step by step: 'Start at −2, subtracting a positive means move left, move 3 steps left, land on −5.' He looks pleased. Siti is paired with a student who has the correct direction. Her partner traces the operation: 'Start at −2, move left 3, land on −5.' Siti watches and copies the number line. But when you ask her 'Why do we move left?', she says 'Because my partner did.' The prompt card is on her desk but she has not read it. You circulate and hear the pattern: 11 pairs are reasoning about direction with clear explanations. 5 pairs are confirming correct answers without questioning the direction. The room has more talk than before, but the depth of reasoning varies. 18 minutes remain.

choices.label: Flex grouping: conceptual pairs share with procedural pairs
choices.elaboration: Identify the 11 pairs who were reasoning about direction clearly. Regroup the class: each reasoning pair joins with one procedural pair to form a group of 4. Ask the reasoning pair to explain their number line reasoning to the procedural pair. The procedural pair must ask at least one clarifying question: 'Why do we move left when we subtract?' or 'What would happen if we moved right?' Circulate and listen for whether the explanations deepen the procedural pairs' understanding.
choices.tradeoff: Flex grouping extends the ZPD scaffolding by pairing students who can reason about direction with students who are still procedural. It gives the reasoning pairs a cognitively demanding task — teaching is harder than doing. But the procedural pairs may ask surface questions or just copy the number line without engaging with the direction reasoning. Siti, who is still copying without understanding, may be overwhelmed in a group of 4 and remain passive.

choices.label: Solo number line check: operation, direction, answer
choices.elaboration: Stop the pair work and ask all students to work individually. Write on the board: 'For the next question: (1) Draw the number line. (2) Mark your starting point. (3) Write the direction you will move and why. (4) Trace the movement and write the answer.' Give 3 minutes, then collect the responses. Scan them to see who has genuine understanding of the direction rule from the pair work and who was just borrowing their partner's reasoning.
choices.tradeoff: Moving from pair talk to solo number line work gives you individual evidence of understanding. It reveals whether the pair work built genuine learning or just social agreement. But it kills the emerging talk momentum in the room. Siti, who did not engage during pair work, will likely produce a blank or copied number line — revealing that the pair work did not help her understand the direction rule.

C.consequence: The Accountable Talk stems go up on the board and pairs begin using them. The room gets louder — not noisy, but noticeably less silent than before. Rajan uses the stem with his partner: 'I claim the answer is −5 because I started at −2 and moved left 3 steps. On the number line, I moved left because subtracting a positive means moving in the negative direction.' His partner responds: 'I agree because that matches the number line.' The exchange is correct but feels scripted. Nur is paired with another student who also got the answer right. They use the stems: 'I claim the answer is −5 because I subtracted.' 'I agree because that is what I got.' The stems are being used, but the direction reasoning is absent — they are confirming the answer, not explaining the movement. Wei Jie is paired with a student who has the same uncertainty about direction. They use the stems: 'I claim the answer is −5 because...' Wei Jie's partner pauses, then says 'because that is what I calculated.' Neither student explains the direction. Siti mumbles 'I agree' but does not offer her own claim. You circulate and hear the pattern: 6 pairs are using the stems to make genuine claims with direction reasoning. 12 pairs are using the stems but staying shallow — confirming answers without explaining direction. 6 pairs are stuck in near-silence or echoing. The stems have activated some talk, but much of it is compliance without depth. 17 minutes remain.

choices.label: AQS (Affirm-Question-Suggest) gallery critique on direction claims
choices.elaboration: Ask each pair to write their Accountable Talk claim on a sticky note: 'I claim the answer is ___ because ___. On the number line, I moved ___ because ___.' Post four notes on the board: one with clear direction reasoning, one with answer-only ('because I subtracted'), one with a direction error, one with no direction explanation. Run a whole-class AQS session on the answer-only note: Affirm what they attempted ('They used the stem structure and got the right answer'), Question what is missing ('Why did you move in that direction? What does subtracting mean on the number line?'), Suggest how to deepen it ('Try adding: I moved left because subtracting a positive means moving in the negative direction'). Ask all pairs to revise their sticky notes.
choices.tradeoff: The AQS gallery critique makes the difference between answer-confirmation and direction reasoning visible to the whole class. It gives the shallow pairs a concrete model of what genuine direction reasoning looks like. But the pairs whose notes are displayed may feel exposed. Siti's pair will likely produce a shallow or incorrect note, and the AQS feedback may feel abstract to students who have not yet articulated the direction rule.

choices.label: Whole-class reteach: direction rule with number line
choices.elaboration: Stop the pair work and address the shallow direction reasoning directly with the whole class. Draw a number line on the board and work through two examples: '−2 − 3: start at −2, subtracting a positive means moving left, move 3 steps left, land on −5.' Then: '−2 + 3: start at −2, adding a positive means moving right, move 3 steps right, land on +1.' Emphasise: 'The direction depends on the operation and the sign of the number you are adding or subtracting — not on whether the starting number is negative.' Ask all students to re-attempt the last question using the number line and write one sentence explaining their direction.
choices.tradeoff: The whole-class reteach with number line examples is efficient and will reach the 12 pairs who were confirming answers without explaining direction. It directly addresses Siti's direction misconception. But it shifts the room back to silence and teacher-led instruction. The students who were starting to reason aloud in pairs lose that momentum. Rajan, who already understands, is under-challenged.

A1.outcome: You pair Rajan with Nur and ask Rajan to give AQS feedback on Nur's diagnostic response. Rajan looks at Nur's paper and begins quietly: 'You got the right answer — −5 is correct. But you said you didn't use a number line. Can you show me the direction on the number line?' Nur draws a number line and traces the movement: 'Start at −2, move left 3, land on −5.' Rajan nods: 'Good — and why left?' Nur pauses, then says: 'Because subtracting means moving left.' Rajan confirms. Wei Jie is paired with a student who chose option (a) and can explain the direction. His partner gives AQS feedback: 'You got the right answer. But you said you didn't always use the number line — try tracing this one.' Wei Jie traces the movement and confirms his answer. Siti is paired with a student who got both questions right. Her partner gives AQS feedback: 'Your answer is wrong — you moved right instead of left. Subtracting means moving left on the number line.' Siti redraws her number line and gets the correct answer. But when you ask her 'Why do we move left?', she says 'Because my partner said so.' On a follow-up check, 17 of 20 unsure students can now explain the direction rule, up from 9. The AQS feedback has activated more talk and improved direction reasoning for the middle group. But Siti is still dependent on copying, and Rajan is under-challenged. 14 minutes remain.
A1.tradeoff: AQS feedback gives quiet students structured practice in articulating direction reasoning. The Affirm-Question-Suggest structure ensures feedback is specific and actionable. Nur and Wei Jie made clear gains through peer support. But Siti's partner corrected her error without building genuine understanding — Siti can now produce the correct answer but cannot explain the direction rule independently. Rajan is giving rather than receiving.
A1.reflection:
Why did you choose AQS peer feedback over whole-class discussion? What were you hoping would happen?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Nur and Wei Jie made gains, but Siti is still copying and Rajan is under-challenged. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for productive peer feedback in quiet classes?

How do you build a classroom culture where students speak with confidence about mathematical reasoning, not just compliance?

A2.outcome: You select Rajan and two other students who got both diagnostic questions right and can explain their direction reasoning. You ask the whole class: 'Why do we move left when we subtract? What is the reasoning?' Rajan responds first (Initiation): 'Because subtracting a positive number means moving in the negative direction — and negative is to the left on the number line.' You nod and ask a second student to build on this (Response): 'So the direction depends on the operation — adding means moving right, subtracting means moving left, regardless of whether the starting number is positive or negative.' A third student evaluates (Feedback): 'Yes — and you can check by counting: −2 − 3 means start at −2 and count 3 steps left: −3, −4, −5.' The IRF chain produces a complete, detailed explanation. You write the final reasoning on the board and ask all students to copy it into their notes. Nur listens carefully and writes the explanation down. Wei Jie copies the board notes. Siti copies the board notes mechanically. When you ask her 'Does this make sense?', she nods quickly but does not speak. Her next answer is still wrong — she has copied the explanation but not corrected her direction error. The IRF chain has made the reasoning visible and public, but it is driven by the 3 students who spoke. The other 21 students are passive observers. 14 minutes remain.
A2.tradeoff: The IRF chain builds a shared, class-constructed explanation that is richer and more detailed than any single student would produce. It makes the direction reasoning visible and models what good justification looks like. Nur and Wei Jie heard a clear explanation. But the chain is driven by the students who are already confident. The majority of quiet students remain passive observers. Siti copied the explanation but did not correct her direction error — the reasoning belongs to the few who spoke, not to everyone.
A2.reflection:
Why did you choose IRF chains over peer feedback? What were you hoping the class would learn?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? The reasoning is now visible, but only 3 students spoke and Siti is still making direction errors. What does this tell you about who does the thinking in an IRF chain?

How do you design whole-class discussion so that quiet students participate and correct their errors, not just observe and copy?

B1.outcome: You regroup the class into groups of 4: each reasoning pair joins with one procedural pair. You ask the reasoning pairs to explain their number line reasoning to the procedural pairs. The procedural pairs must ask at least one clarifying question. Rajan's group begins. Rajan explains: 'Start at −2, subtract 3 means move left 3 steps, land on −5. We move left because subtracting a positive means moving in the negative direction.' The procedural pair asks: 'Why is negative to the left?' Rajan responds: 'Because the number line goes from negative on the left to positive on the right — it is a convention.' The procedural pair nods and writes this down. Nur's group works similarly — the reasoning pair explains, the procedural pair asks a clarifying question, and the explanation deepens. Wei Jie is in a group where the reasoning pair explains clearly, but Wei Jie's procedural pair asks surface questions: 'Is this right? Is that right?' without engaging with the direction reasoning. Siti is in a group of 4 but remains silent. When the reasoning pair explains, Siti nods but does not ask a question. When prompted by you, she says quietly: 'I don't know what to ask.' The group continues without her. On a follow-up check, 16 of 24 students can now explain the direction rule, up from 11. 14 minutes remain.
B1.tradeoff: Flex grouping extends the ZPD scaffolding by pairing students who can reason about direction with students who are still procedural. It gives the reasoning pairs a cognitively demanding task — teaching is harder than doing. Some procedural pairs asked genuine clarifying questions and deepened their understanding. But the procedural pairs often dominated the groups by asking surface questions. Siti remained passive and did not engage with the direction reasoning.
B1.reflection:
Why did you choose flex grouping over solo work? What were you hoping would happen?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Some groups reasoned deeply, but others stayed procedural and Siti remained passive. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for productive group work in quiet classes?

How do you ensure that all students in a group are actively reasoning about direction, not just observing or copying?

B2.outcome: You stop the pair work and ask all students to work individually on the solo number line check. The room goes silent again. Rajan draws a clear number line with all four steps labelled and writes: 'I moved left because subtracting a positive means moving in the negative direction. I check by counting: −2, −3, −4, −5 = −5 ✓.' His response shows deep understanding and metacognitive awareness. Nur draws the number line correctly and writes: 'I moved left because subtracting means going negative. I check by counting back.' Her response shows she has internalised the direction rule from the pair work. Wei Jie draws the number line and traces the movement correctly. His direction explanation says: 'I moved left because the prompt card says subtracting a positive means move left.' He is still dependent on the prompt card rather than the conceptual rule. Siti draws a number line but moves in the wrong direction again — she writes: 'I moved right because −2 is negative.' Her response reveals that the pair work did not correct her direction misconception. The solo check has given you honest individual evidence. Rajan and Nur have genuine understanding; Wei Jie is dependent on the prompt; Siti still has the direction misconception. 14 minutes remain.
B2.tradeoff: Solo number line work gives you clean, individual evidence of understanding — uncontaminated by pair talk. Rajan and Nur demonstrated genuine understanding of the direction rule. But Wei Jie is still dependent on the prompt card, and Siti's direction misconception has not been corrected by the pair work. The move to solo work killed the emerging talk momentum. You have clear evidence of the gap — but limited time to address it.
B2.reflection:
Why did you choose to move from pair talk to solo number line work? What were you trying to find out?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan and Nur showed genuine understanding, but Wei Jie is prompt-dependent and Siti still has the direction misconception. What does this tell you about when pair talk builds genuine understanding versus borrowed reasoning?

How do you correct a specific direction misconception like Siti's — where the student associates negative numbers with rightward movement — without making her feel singled out in a quiet class?

C1.outcome: Four sticky notes are posted on the board. You run the AQS session on the answer-only note: 'I claim the answer is −5 because I subtracted.' Several students affirm: 'They used the stem structure and got the right answer.' You write this on the board. Then you ask: 'What should we question?' Nur raises her hand tentatively: 'They didn't explain the direction. Why did they move left?' You write: 'Question: Why did you move left? What does subtracting mean on the number line?' A third student suggests: 'Add: I moved left because subtracting a positive means moving in the negative direction.' You write: 'Suggest: Explain the direction — subtracting = move left.' The AQS feedback is now complete and visible. You ask all pairs to revise their sticky notes. Several shallow pairs add direction reasoning to their notes for the first time. Wei Jie's pair revises their note to include 'moved left because subtracting.' Siti's pair produces a revised note that says 'moved left' — correct direction — but when you ask Siti to demonstrate on a new question, she moves right again. The AQS process has corrected the surface claim but not the underlying misconception. 14 minutes remain.
C1.tradeoff: The AQS gallery critique made the direction reasoning visible and gave the shallow pairs a concrete model of what genuine reasoning looks like. Several pairs added direction reasoning to their notes for the first time. But Siti's direction misconception is not corrected by the AQS process — she can now write 'moved left' on a sticky note but still moves right when she calculates. The AQS feedback served the middle group but did not address the most fundamental error in the room.
C1.reflection:
Why did you choose to use the gallery talk output as the basis for AQS feedback? What were you hoping the class would learn?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Several pairs added direction reasoning, but Siti's misconception remains uncorrected. What does this tell you about the limits of whole-class feedback when a student has a specific, persistent misconception?

How do you address a direction misconception like Siti's in a quiet class — where students are reluctant to speak and errors are hidden behind correct-looking answers?

C2.outcome: The room goes quiet as students write their solo number line proofs. Rajan draws a clear, labelled number line with all four steps and writes: 'I moved left because subtracting a positive means moving in the negative direction. The number line goes negative to the left and positive to the right — this is a convention, not a rule to memorise.' His proof shows deep understanding and metacognitive awareness. Nur draws the number line correctly and writes: 'I moved left because subtracting means going negative. I check by counting.' Her proof is correct and clear. Wei Jie draws the number line and traces the movement correctly, but his proof says: 'I moved left because the prompt card says so.' He is still dependent on the external rule rather than the conceptual understanding. Siti draws a number line and moves right — her proof says: 'I moved right because −2 is negative and negative is on the left, so I go right.' Her reasoning reveals the specific misconception: she is confusing the position of negative numbers on the number line with the direction of movement. The solo proofs have given you the clearest, most honest picture of each student's understanding. Rajan and Nur have genuine conceptual understanding; Wei Jie has the procedure but not the concept; Siti has a specific, articulable misconception that the pair talk did not correct. 14 minutes remain.
C2.tradeoff: Solo written proofs give you the clearest, most honest picture of each student's understanding of the direction rule. Rajan and Nur demonstrated genuine conceptual understanding. But the move to individual work has stopped the emerging talk. Wei Jie's proof reveals he is prompt-dependent. Siti's proof reveals a specific, articulable misconception — she is confusing the position of negative numbers with the direction of movement — that the pair talk did not surface or correct.
C2.reflection:
Why did you choose to move from pair talk to solo written proofs? What were you trying to find out?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan and Nur showed genuine understanding, but Wei Jie is prompt-dependent and Siti's misconception is now clearly articulated. What does this tell you about when pair talk surfaces misconceptions versus when it hides them?

Now that you can see Siti's specific misconception — confusing position with direction — how would you address it? What would you say to her, and how would you design the next task to correct it?
